content.assessment: This proposal directly targets the structural mechanism the lens identifies as central: the multi-stage selection funnel. Delaying selection to age 14 removes two of three selection points, which the lens evidence directly implicates in stratification. However, the lens explicitly warns that whole-system gains (as in Portugal) cannot be attributed to any single isolated intervention; this proposal is structural but not obviously coordinated with quality-levelling or equity-support measures, which the lens frames as necessary complements rather than substitutes for structural change.
opportunities: Could be sequenced with compensatory quality investment (as in other proposals) to constitute the kind of coordinated reform bundle the lens deems necessary
Provides a structural foundation onto which admissions and fiscal reforms could later be layered
strengths: Directly eliminates two of the three selection points identified as constituting the stratifying funnel
Aligns Hungary's tracking age with the OECD-typical age, addressing the structural anomaly identified as unusual within its nominally single-structure system
threats: Absent complementary equity and quality-levelling measures, structural reform alone may replicate the pattern where administrative changes produce only partial, stagnating effects on segregation
Risk that the reform becomes an isolated structural act, defeating causal clarity about which lever produced any observed change
weaknesses: Structural change alone, absent a bundled quality-levelling and equity strategy, may be insufficient per the lens's core caution against attributing systemic gains to isolated interventions
No mechanism in the proposal addresses the SES-linked selection dynamics documented independent of track entry age itself
